segments.title: Three Victorian naval diplomacy crises
segments.summary: A short introduction frames Constantinople, Alexandria, and Tangier as three Mediterranean case studies in Victorian naval diplomacy between 1878 and 1906.
segments.body: The video sets up a comparative look at Constantinople, Alexandria, and the Tangier Crisis—incidents spread across 1878–1906. Together they introduce the question of how naval forces could be used to signal resolve and shape a political crisis.

evidence.note: The presenter introduces naval diplomacy through Constantinople, Alexandria, and Tangier, dating the cases to 1878–1906.

segments.title: When naval pressure succeeds—or escalates
segments.summary: The comparison distinguishes a crisis that turned violent, muddled handling that worsened others, and a coordinated response that achieved the least damaging result.
segments.body: The deciding factor is not naval presence alone, but how political direction and commanders on the scene interact. Prompt action by an officer can force a decision, while local naval realities can worsen a crisis; clear guidance and common purpose between diplomacy and the navy offer the least damaging outcome.

evidence.note: The presenter contrasts a conflict that became hot, muddled handling, decisive local action, and a case where naval and diplomatic plans worked together.
